22: Isaiah’s oracle against Sennacherib — ʾet mî ḥēraptā ûḇizzāltā wᵉʿal mî hărîmōtā qôl wattissāʾ mārôm ʿêneyḵā ʿal qᵉḏôš yiśrāʾēl, ‘Whom have you mocked and reviled? Against whom have you raised your voice and lifted your eyes to the heights? Against the Holy One of Israel!’ — centers the oracle on the divine title qᵉḏôš yiśrāʾēl (Holy One of Israel). This is Isaiah’s characteristic title for YHWH, appearing 25 times in the book of Isaiah (compared to 6 times in all the rest of the OT). The title fuses holiness (qōḏeš) — the absolute otherness and transcendence of YHWH, the unapproachable divine quality that fills the seraphim’s hymn in Isa 6:3 (qāḏôš qāḏôš qāḏôš) — with covenant particularity (‘of Israel’). Sennacherib has raised his voice against the one who cannot be approached without dying (Isa 6:5: ‘woe is me! I am undone’); his doom is not military defeat but the offense of approaching the unapproachably holy without the mediation that YHWH alone provides. Rev 4:8 takes the seraphic triple-holy into the heavenly court where the four living creatures hymn the eternal God; the NT resolution of holiness is not distance but mediation — the blood of Christ enabling approach to the holy (Heb 10:19: parrēsian eis tēn eisodon tōn hagiōn, ‘confidence to enter the holy places’).
1: Hezekiah's immediate response is the full penitential form: tearing clothes (wayyiqraʿ ʾet-bᵉgāḏāyw) + sackcloth (wayyitkaś baśśāq) + going to the temple. The sequence parallels David's and Josiah's responses to crisis. Going to the temple is not merely religious observance — it is a physical act of bringing the crisis into YHWH's presence, placing it before the one who alone can resolve it.
2: Hezekiah sends to Isaiah — "the prophet, the son of Amoz" — the full designation signals formal prophetic consultation. The delegation includes Eliakim, Shebna, and the senior priests, all in sackcloth — the court models the royal penitential posture. Isaiah functions here in his role as court prophet, the institutional mediator between royal crisis and divine response.
3: "A day of distress, rebuke, and disgrace" — ṣārāh wᵉtôkāḥat ûnᵉʾāṣāh — three nouns of acute crisis. The "children come to the opening of the womb, but there is no strength to give birth" — bānîm bāʾû ʿad-mōṣāʾ wᵉḵōaḥ ʾayin lᵉlēdāh. The birth metaphor for crisis is used elsewhere in the psalms (Ps 48:6) and prophets (Isa 37:3). The image is of labor that reaches the moment of crisis but cannot complete — maximum vulnerability with no ability to deliver.
4: ʾĕlōhîm ḥayyîm — "the living God" — this is the theological counter to the Rabshakeh's catalog of dead national deities (18:33–35). By naming YHWH as "living," Hezekiah's message to Isaiah frames the crisis as a category error on the Rabshakeh's part: he has compared YHWH to gods who cannot speak, act, or hear. "Perhaps YHWH your God will hear" — the ʾûlay (perhaps) is not uncertainty about YHWH's power but the proper humility of a petitioner.
5: A brief transitional verse — the servants' arrival at Isaiah. The narrator's economy here contrasts with the speech elaboration of ch18; the prophetic response will be correspondingly immediate. No extended negotiation is needed because Isaiah already has a word from YHWH.
6: Isaiah's oracle of salvation (ôrākle de salut) opens with the standard formula ʾal-tîrāʾ — "do not be afraid" — the reassurance formula that marks prophetic oracles of divine intervention throughout Isaiah (Isa 7:4; 10:24; 37:6; 41:10). "The words you have heard" (haddᵉḇārîm ʾăšer šāmaʿtā) — YHWH validates that he has heard the Rabshakeh's speech; the words against the holy one have reached YHWH's ears.
7: "I am going to put a spirit in him" — nōtēn bô rûaḥ — YHWH acts directly on Sennacherib's perception. Rûaḥ (H7307) here is a psychological/perceptual agent — a disposition, report, or impulse that will redirect him. "He will hear a report" (wᵉšāmaʿ šᵉmûʿāh) — šᵉmûʿāh (H8052) is "news, tidings" — the Tirhakah report (v9) is the fulfillment. "Fall by the sword in his own land" — the warrior boaster dies not in siege but in his own temple (v37), killed by his own sons.
8: The Rabshakeh returns to find Sennacherib fighting at Libnah — the narrative gap explains why Sennacherib could not advance on Jerusalem immediately. Lachish is now taken; the campaign shifts. The transitional verses (8–9) show Sennacherib still active in Judah while the divine response is being prepared.
9: Tirhakah king of Ethiopia (Cush) — historically Taharqa, who became Pharaoh in 690 BC but was involved in anti-Assyrian coalition politics earlier. The news of his approach gives Sennacherib reason to intensify his Jerusalem diplomatic pressure. "He sent messengers again to Hezekiah" — the second letter follows the same pattern as the speech but in writing (v14).
10: The second Assyrian message deploys the same theological argument: "Do not let your God, in whom you are trusting, deceive you" — ʾal-yašśiʾkā ʾĕlōhêkā ʾăšer-ʾattāh bōṭēaḥ bô. Bāṭaḥ appears again — the Assyrian diplomacy consistently targets Hezekiah's trust as the vulnerability. The argument: Jerusalem's deliverance is a deception YHWH is perpetrating on Hezekiah, not a promise he can keep.
11: "You have heard what the kings of Assyria have done to all the countries" — the empirical survey returns. ḥᵉrēm (H2763) is used — "utterly destroying them" — the devotion-to-destruction term. The letter rehearses the same inductive argument: every nation destroyed, you will be no different. The shift from speech to letter is significant; it gives the argument a more formal, documented character.
12: The catalog of defeated nations and their gods: Gozan (on the Habor River in Assyria), Haran (ancestral Abrahamic site), Rezeph (on the Euphrates), Eden (Beth-Eden, an Aramean kingdom). "The gods of those nations did not deliver them" — the premise is identical to 18:33–35. The Assyrian archive of conquest is offered as the decisive proof against YHWH's ability to intervene.
13: A rhetorical question — "Where are the kings of Hamath, Arpad, Sepharvaim, Hena, and Ivvah?" — answers itself: they are gone, deposed, their cities destroyed. The past tense of defeat is the tense of certainty about Jerusalem's future, in Sennacherib's logic. The cities listed span from Northern Syria to the Euphrates, representing the full sweep of Assyrian conquest.
14: Hezekiah "received the letter" (wayyiqqaḥ … ʾet-hassᵉpārîm) and "spread it out before the LORD" (wayyipprᵉśēhû liphᵉnê YHWH). The physical act of laying the threatening letter before YHWH in the temple is remarkable — it is the anti-prayer version of bringing complaints to the judge. Hezekiah presents the written threat as evidence, letting YHWH see what has been said. This is prayer through enacted metaphor: the one who reads all things is asked to read the enemy's letter.
15: The prayer's opening invocation — "O LORD, God of Israel, enthroned between the cherubim" (YHWH ʾĕlōhê yiśrāʾēl yōšēḇ hakkᵉrûḇîm) — uses the throne-room epithet also in Ps 80:1; 99:1; 1 Sam 4:4. The cherubim are the ark's guardians; this is YHWH as present in his earthly throne room in the temple, not an absent deity. "You alone are God over all the kingdoms of the earth" (ʾattāh-hûʾ hāʾĕlōhîm lᵉḇaḏᵉkā lᵉkōl mamlᵉkôt hāʾāreṣ) — the universal sovereignty claim directly counters the Rabshakeh's "which of the gods of the nations." YHWH is not one among them.
16: "Incline your ear, LORD, and hear; open your eyes, LORD, and see" — the anthropomorphic petition uses the physical senses of hearing and sight to invoke divine attentiveness. The double address (YHWH … YHWH) and the two imperatives (haṭṭēh … pᵉqaḥ) are the lament form's call for divine action. "Words Sennacherib has sent to mock the living God" (ḥērap ʾet-ʾĕlōhîm ḥay) — returns the ḥayyîm (living) epithet from v4, making mockery of the living God the central charge against Sennacherib.
17: Hezekiah concedes the historical fact: "It is true, LORD, that the kings of Assyria have destroyed these nations and their lands." The concession is rhetorically significant — Hezekiah's prayer is honest. He does not deny the evidence; he reframes its meaning. The Assyrian conquests were real, but the explanation for them is theological, not testimonial of YHWH's weakness.
18: "They have thrown their gods into the fire — for those were no gods at all, merely human handiwork, wood and stone" (lōʾ ʾĕlōhîm hēmmāh kî ʾim-maʿăśēh yᵉḏê-ʾāḏām ʿēṣ wāʾeḇen wayyᵃʾaḇᵉḏûm). The theological axiom: the defeated "gods" were no gods — they were human artifacts that could be burned. The destruction of national deities by Assyria proves only that idols cannot survive fire, not that YHWH cannot survive Sennacherib. The argument dismantles the Rabshakeh's induction by distinguishing categories of deity.
19: "Now, O LORD our God, deliver us from his hand" — the petition follows the theological argument: having established that YHWH is categorically different from the national deities, Hezekiah asks for the act that will demonstrate it. "So that all the kingdoms of the earth may know that you, LORD, are God alone" (lᵉmaʿan yēḏᵉʿû kōl mamlᵉkôt hāʾāreṣ kî ʾattāh YHWH ʾĕlōhîm lᵉḇaḏᵉkā) — the witness formula. Deliverance is not merely self-preservation; it is testimony. The nations need to know what YHWH is, and only an act of deliverance can demonstrate it.
20: Isaiah's response — "I have heard your prayer" (šāmaʿtî) — uses the same šāmaʿ root that the Rabshakeh used to taunt ("did I come up without the LORD saying…?"). YHWH has heard, in contrast to the gods who could not hear (18:34–35). The messenger formula — "This is what the LORD, the God of Israel, says" — frames what follows as divine speech, not Isaiah's editorial comment.
21: "The virgin daughter of Zion despises and mocks you; the daughter of Jerusalem wags her head behind you" — bᵉtûlat bat-ṣiyyôn is the feminine personification of Jerusalem as YHWH's city. The mocking gesture — wagging the head (nāʿăh rōʾš) — is an inversion of the Rabshakeh's own mockery (18:27). Jerusalem is not the terrified city Sennacherib imagines; she laughs at him. The oracle opens with contempt before moving to judgment.
23: "Through your messengers you have taunted the Lord" — bᵉyaḏ malʾākêkā ḥēraptā ʾăḏōnāy — the charge is not against Hezekiah but directly against Sennacherib's own speech. "With my many chariots I climbed to the top of the mountains" — the boast of Assyrian military engineering. "I will cut down its tallest cedars" — the Lebanon cedar was the symbol of imperial reach and pride. The imagery is inverted: the one who boasts of conquest is addressed as the object of YHWH's judgment.
24: "I dug wells and drank foreign water; with the soles of my feet I dried up all the streams of Egypt" — the hyperbolic self-praise of the Assyrian conqueror, who claims even to have altered the hydrology of Egypt. The image of drying the Nile's branches with footsteps is military boasting in its most grandiose form. YHWH's oracle quotes these words back, implicitly to expose their absurdity.
25: "Have you not heard? I determined this long ago; from ancient times I planned it" — the divine sovereignty claim that Sennacherib's conquests were YHWH's prior plan, not Sennacherib's achievement. mêrāḥôq yāṣartîhā — "from afar I formed it" — the verb yāṣar (H3335, to form, shape as a potter) is the same verb used of God forming humanity (Gen 2:7). Sennacherib's victories were YHWH's pottery, not Sennacherib's conquest. The great empires are instruments of divine purpose, not autonomous powers.
26: "That is why their inhabitants had so little power — they were shattered and ashamed" — the passive victims of Assyrian conquest were so only because YHWH had predestined it. ḥattû wāḇōšû — shattered and ashamed — the vocabulary of defeat in its full humiliation. The "plants of the field … grass on the rooftops" simile (kᵉḏešeʾ śādeh ûkᵉyereq deśeʾ ḥāṣîr gaggôt) — the conquests as grass that sprouts and withers quickly, rootless and ephemeral before YHWH's plans.
27: "But I know where you live and when you come and go, and I know your rage against me" (wᵉyōšbᵉkā wᵉṣēʾtkā ûbōʾekā yāḏaʿtî). YHWH's omniscience over Sennacherib is total: his residence, his movements, his inner rage (rigzô). The contrast with the Rabshakeh's taunt — "the LORD said to me, Go up" — is complete: it was not Sennacherib directing YHWH but YHWH directing Sennacherib, and now YHWH knows all of it including the boast.
28: "Because your fury against me and your arrogance have reached my ears, I will put my hook in your nose and my bridle in your mouth" — wᵉśamtî ḥaḥî bᵉʾappekā ûmitgî bᵉśᵉpātêkā. The hook-in-nose and bridle imagery is precisely what Assyria did to conquered peoples; Assyrian palace reliefs show captive kings led with hooks through their lips. YHWH turns Sennacherib's own imperial apparatus on him — the instruments of domination become the instruments of his humiliation. "I will make you go back the same way you came" — the retreat is YHWH's direction, not Sennacherib's choice.
29: The sign for Hezekiah is agricultural: this year (hašššānāh) eat what grows on its own (sāpîaḥ), second year what springs from that (šāḵîš), third year plant and harvest (zirʿû wiqṣōrû). The three-year timeline is precise. The sign is not dramatic but patient: the land's agricultural restoration over time, showing that YHWH's deliverance is not a one-time miracle but a new normal of security. "Growing on its own" presupposes disruption of normal agriculture — the siege year had prevented planting.
30: "The surviving remnant of the house of Judah will again take root downward and bear fruit upward" — wᵉhōsîpāh pᵉlêṭat bêt-yᵉhûdāh hannišʾāret šōreš lᵉmattāh wᵉʿāśāh pᵉrî lᵉmaʿlāh. The remnant (pᵉlêṭāh) motif is Isaianic — a theological category that runs through the book. "Take root downward" is the image of organic stability; "bear fruit upward" is the image of flourishing. The reversal from crisis to fruitfulness is described in botanical terms, quietly but profoundly.
31: qinʾat YHWH ṣᵉḇāʾôt — "the zeal of the LORD of hosts" (H7068) will accomplish this. Qinʾāh is divine passion/jealousy — the same burning drive that YHWH has for exclusive covenant relationship and for his own honor. It is not sentiment but energy; it is what moves YHWH to act on behalf of his remnant against the empire. "The LORD of hosts" (YHWH ṣᵉḇāʾôt) — the title of YHWH as commander of the heavenly armies, especially relevant when military power is the visible threat.
32: The fourfold guarantee against Sennacherib entering Jerusalem: will not enter this city / will not shoot an arrow here / will not come before it with a shield / will not pour a siege ramp against it. Each element represents a standard Assyrian siege tactic. The list is technically precise — the four methods are exactly what siege armies would use. YHWH's prohibition is equally precise: none of these tactics will be employed against Jerusalem.
33: "He will go back the same way he came" — the reversal formula. YHWH's declaration reverses the direction of the invasion. ûbaḏderek ʾăšer-bāʾ bāh yāšûḇ — the exact path of ingress becomes the path of retreat. The Deuteronomic blessing and curse structures have a similar reversal pattern; blessing and cursing are directional. "Declares the LORD" (nᵉʾum-YHWH) — the prophetic speech-act formula sealing the oracle.
34: "I will defend this city and save it, for my own sake and for the sake of my servant David" — the dual motivation is theologically significant. lᵉmaʿănî (for my own sake) — YHWH's honor is at stake; the Rabshakeh's taunt against YHWH is answered by YHWH's action. ûlᵉmaʿan dāwid ʿaḇdî (for the sake of my servant David) — the Davidic covenant is the other ground; Jerusalem's protection is a covenant obligation, not merely a display of power. These two motivations together span divine honor and covenant faithfulness.
35: malʾak YHWH — the angel of the LORD as divine warrior. "That night" (wayᵉhî ballaylāh hahûʾ) — the night setting for divine intervention is consistent with the Passover and Exodus typology. "A hundred and eighty-five thousand men in the Assyrian camp" — mēʾāh šᵉmōnîm wᵉḥāmêšāh ʾelep. The number is large and has been debated; Assyrian annals often overcount victories. Whatever the historical event, the narrative is clear: the morning reveals mass death without battle (kullām pᵉḡārîm mētîm — "they were all dead bodies"). The divine warrior wins without Judahite military action — YHWH alone defends Jerusalem.
36: Sennacherib "broke camp and withdrew and returned to Nineveh, where he stayed" — the six verbs of departure (wayyisaʿ wayyēlek wayyāšob etc.) narrate his exit with blunt economy. The boaster of 18:33–35 simply goes home. The contrast between the elaborate rhetorical assault and the bare narrative of retreat is the text's understated triumph. His annals, by contrast, claim a great victory — the propagandistic gap is historically instructive.
37: Sennacherib is killed by his own sons Adrammelech and Sharezer while "worshipping in the house of Nisroch his god" (bêt nisrōk ʾĕlōhāyw). The irony is complete: the man who mocked YHWH by comparing him to powerless national deities (18:33–35) dies in the temple of one of those deities, at the hands of his own sons. "Nisroch" is not securely identified; possibly a form of Nusku or Marduk. "They escaped to the land of Ararat" — Urartu (modern Armenia) — corroborated in Assyrian and Babylonian records. Esarhaddon reigned in his place — the dynasty continues, but Sennacherib himself falls by the sword in his own land, exactly as YHWH predicted (v7).
